sentences.text: You know, I remember one time I was going from school.
And I heard people playing music.
You know, in Poland, the essence was more-- the orthodox families was more on the spiritual, not so much on the physical.
So in fact, I remember that my husband told me that he said once that he would like to play violin.
And his father said, [NON-ENGLISH] what?
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] My son's going to be a [NON-ENGLISH] no way.
And it's ironic, because my son now plays music.
But-- this is not the time.
But what I wanted to say about music, so it was not so much appreciated.
The arts wasn't that time for Hasidic people now.
But anyhow, there were a lot of people who to make a living would go on the yard from yard to yard and play music.
And some of them very good.
And I knew all the modern Polish songs, and Yiddish songs.
And they were singing in Yiddish.
They were going from place to place.
And people would listen and throw them down coins.
You know?
And they sang a new song.
And I was so anxious to learn that song.
And I had a good memory, so I caught most of it.
A few words were missing.
So I figured they're probably not going to play at the same courtyard twice the same song, so I'm going to follow them.
It was a Friday.
I was going from school.
They walked from place, to place, to place.
And I'm waiting and waiting until they play the song.
All of a sudden I realize it's getting dark.
You know?
I said, oh my gosh, it's going to be Shabbas.
The family's going to be at the table.
I'm coming home, everybody at the Shabbas table.
Everything is set and everything beautiful.
And my mother looks at me, [?
Itkala ?], quick.
Go in, wash up and change.
It's Shabbas already.
You know?
And I was so taken in with these songs, with the music.
I was so eager always to learn.
And this is why I suffered so much when later they did not permit us to do all the things that we were allowed to do.
Yiddish songs?
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SINGING IN YIDDISH]
OK.
[SPEAKING YIDDISH] The vanished world, what once was, is no more.
[SPEAKING YIDDISH] The face gets wrinkled.
The hair is grey.
You dress up, you makeup, you try to look young.
But you don't fool anybody but yourself.
Today, you are a child who's still in the cradle.
Tomorrow, you're a bride.
Then the [INAUDIBLE], and then I get mother, and my grandmother.
And [INAUDIBLE] already marches on.
So what's to worry?
What once was is no more.
So what do you have to worry about yesterday to lose today's joy and happiness?
What do you have to worry about tomorrow?
About-- tomorrow is still not here.
It's just today.
So be happy, content, and laugh, and enjoy as long as your day, today is yours.
Yesterday will never come back.
And tomorrow, only God knows what will be.
You were talking about Shabbas before.
What was Shabbas like in the house?
Shabbas.
The preparation for Shabbas started on Thursday, going to buy fish, live carp.
We put it in a-- in a wooden tub.
You know?
Go to the market, buy all the things, the baking, the cooking.
On Friday, we'd go and take the [INAUDIBLE] to the baker.
[INAUDIBLE] is a dish of potatoes, and meat, and beans, or whatever.
And you take it Friday to bring it to the baker, because you don't cook on Shabbas.
And on Saturday, you go to the baker.
It's [INAUDIBLE] you bring it after-- you-- the poorest families were rich on Shabbas.
Because you would prepare, and you would save all things.
You try to have traditional meals.
You go to shul.
You dress up.
Shabbas was a day of rest.
Usually when the-- when the-- my father, before he went to shul, first he went to shul.
We lit the candle for went to shul.
He-- he used to give everybody a hug and blessed us, and went to shul.
We came home.
We had a beautiful Friday night traditional meal, you know, with gefilte fish, with chicken soup, and [INAUDIBLE], you know, and all the goodies.
And we binged, and we used to sing songs and used to tell stories.
And whenever dad, my tatesh-- tatesh, we used to call them, mamesh and tatesh in yiddish.
Would be always affectionate-- go to shul.
He would bring home an oyrekh.
An oyrekh is a guest.
A guest, why?
Because there was some yeshivas.
There was only synagogue with yeshivas.
So some boys would come to study.
And they would follow him home.
Also, a lot of poor people stood at the synagogue.
They didn't have enough to eat.
So always we would have a guest for Friday night, would bring home.
And again, would be lots of story telling, you know.
I just, I grew up with stories, you know.
It was wonderful.
And we were singing, we were talking, reminiscing.
It was beautiful.
It was beautiful.
I went to Hebrew school, [?
Beth Yakov ?].
And I went to public school.
The-- the--
No.
Public school-- in fact, I have a picture of this.
I didn't have one single picture when I was liberated.
But I'll tell you later how I got them.
But, anyhow, the public school.
The name of the person on the public school was a Jewish man from Ciechanow.
His name was Kalfus.
K-A-L-F-U-S, Kalfus.
And anyhow-- so we had a very good life.
We were close with the family.
We celebrated the holiday.
We went to vacations.
We've-- Then I wanted to join a [INAUDIBLE].
But, unfortunately, I was told that I'm too young.
I was 12 years old when I wanted to join a [INAUDIBLE].
That's a group.
And the leader from this group, from the young girls, was a girl named [?
Rosa ?] Robato.
Rosa ?] is O-O-S-A.
Robato, which means work, R-O-B-A-T-O.
And she was a very, very devoted Zionist, you know.
And I remember I came, because some of my other friends who were somewhat older joined.
So I wanted to join.
And she said to me, [?
Itkala, ?] you have to come.
You cannot start until you're 13.
And I was 13 years old I started a different-- my life was finished.
What I know about her, that she was a devoted-- she was a very devoted Zionist.
She was dreaming of going to Palestine.
She was-- she was-- I didn't know it was a girlfriend.
Because she was older than I.
She was [INAUDIBLE].
In fact, I have a photograph of her.
It's not an actual photograph, but now after the Holocaust I found something.
Because she became my heroine in the Holocaust.
So I knew her family.
She had a sister, [INAUDIBLE].
I don't-- didn't know her family close.
But I knew her only through this incident, mostly.
But I knew who she was.
And Ciechanow was a small city.
So everybody practically knew each other, you know.
It was a very close community.
And another thing.
There was not-- in Poland, there was no funerals, you know, like funeral parlors for the Jewish people.
We didn't have funeral parlors for the Jewish people.
There was [INAUDIBLE].
It was a special group designated which was volunteers.
And they would prepare the body according to the Jewish laws.
You know, we're not allowed to put on fancy things.
You're suppose to wear [BACKGROUND NOISE] [NON-ENGLISH] they called it in Yiddish, special burial clothes white-- made from white clothes.
They would come, women, to the house and would wash the body, the corpse.
And they would sew and everything, so everything was on a volunteer basis.
You know, there was a [NON-ENGLISH].
There was [NON-ENGLISH].
It was-- even the rabbi was not paid, you know, like the rabbi is here.
But he-- he did, everything was done-- a lot of charity work and everything.
And another thing which I remember, the worst Jewish people didn't go to a civil court.
Everything from whether it's kosher, the meat, if something is, to disputes, they would go and ask the rabbi.
And the rabbi would be the final authority.
Because the rabbi studied all his life Torah, and it went by commandment.
You know, the 613 commandments influenced everything.
So he would-- in fact, there is a beautiful story that I remember about [INAUDIBLE].
Two men had a dispute, two business partners.
And this one claimed he's right and came to the rabbi.
You know, they couldn't agree.
So they didn't go to a civil court to sue.
So they went to the rabbi.
And the rabbi-- it was a community within a community.
You know?
Double taxes you would pay, taxes for charity, for holding up the Jewish institutions, and charity government.
But the [BACKGROUND NOISE] Jewish community was like self-contained.
So anyhow-- so I remember that the story was that two men came for [NON-ENGLISH] That means, for the rabbi to settle dispute.
He listened to both.
And he said, my son, you are right to the first one.
And then he listened to [INAUDIBLE] and said, you are right.
And the-- rabbi's wife standing there listening.
She said, my dear husband, how can both be right when they both disagree?
He said, my wife, you are right, too.
You know?
Which means to tell that from their point of view, everybody's right.
You know?
So the fact that I grew up in a home where Yiddish songs, Yiddish literature, Yiddish sayings, proverbs was quoted constantly.
So in fact, I remember my grandmother had so many Yiddish proverbs and so many folk songs and everything else.
What I love, my grandmother love, I loved the wonderful-- she was a wonderful woman.
And she loved me unconditionally, so did my parents.
But what I loved the foremost is that she never took me for granted.
Whenever-- whenever she asked me to do a little errand for her and I went, she would thank me many, many times and bless me.
Says, [NON-ENGLISH].
And I said, grandmother, it's just a little thing.
And my grandmother used to answer with a smile.
Yes, my child.
But kindness and caring are not just little things.
So it's the values that were expressed in the song and in the literature and everything.
This is what nourished me spiritually.
Yes.
But not too many.
And [INAUDIBLE].
Was one of my friends.
And there was that incident with [INAUDIBLE].
Whenever she would come to our home-- not just [INAUDIBLE], anybody-- my mother would always treat them with food.
And then when I came to her house, I would not take everything, because we were kosher.
So one day [INAUDIBLE] was upset.
And she was actually crying.
She said, Itka, that's not fair.
I eat in your house when your mother treats me.
Why don't you want to eat in my house?
So I went to my mother and actually asked.
I said, how come that-- this is not fair?
I felt that she's right.
And my mother said, yes, you can eat at her house.
You can have a fruit.
But you have dietary laws that we cannot-- prohibit us.
She can eat everything, because her laws are different, she said.
And I said, but that's not fair.
You know, I argued for my-- for her.
And my mother said something that I remembered till this day.
It's little things that you remember.
She said, whose birthday do you celebrate, [INAUDIBLE] or yours?
I said, of course, mine.
Whose birthday does [INAUDIBLE] celebrate?
I said, hers.
So, see, you can share your birthdays, but you cannot celebrate her birthday [? as ?] yours.
So that was a wonderful example for me.
And as far as you hear children sometimes say, why can't we have a Christmas tree?
That never happened to us, because I wasn't-- didn't have that close neighbors to see the Christmas tree.
And we just had our own tradition, and we were satisfied.
But what my mother told me, always have to respect and tolerate others.
So this, always tolerance, was very, very tolerant woman.
Very-- I would describe my family, they exhibited [NON-ENGLISH].
Well, at that time I didn't-- run around with too much.
With [INAUDIBLE] I used to play.
She used to come over to play.
She didn't live too far away.
But I didn't have so many non-Jewish friends.
Because you know, when I went to school, there was mostly Jewish kids.
When I went to Hebrew school, was also Jewish kids, the holidays.
And so it-- there wasn't that much.
But there was no prohibition.
Because with my mother, I told you, she was-- I don't know how to explain.
But because she wasn't Orthodox.
She already was-- when we lived in [INAUDIBLE] when we went to for the summer.
And one time, she was a wonderful dancer.
She had a beautiful voice.
She was very artistic, very full of life.
So one time, they played the polka, which is a Polish very much dance.
One of the peasants grabbed her.
She didn't object.
And she danced.
And he said, [NON-ENGLISH].
He never saw a Jewish woman dancing so well.
Well, most women didn't dance with peasants.
My mother didn't do it habitually.
But she was tolerant to [? get ?] [? to ?] [? dance, she ?] figure it's OK.
But that was the extent of it.
Actually, the two incident that I had was when the-- when the kids beat me up.
It was with the snow and sometimes many remark.
But you see, [INAUDIBLE] when he was alive he was very tolerant.
And Jews have very, very good at that time.
After he died, like 1938, this started to change.
And they would say [NON-ENGLISH SPEAKING].
Don't buy from Jews.
Started to-- OK.
We were never fully accepted even though we lived there for so many years.
We couldn't go to ever to the universities.
We were-- we were always treated like second-class citizens as a general.
Though, it was not the official policy of the government.
But in spite of it, my family managed to live there from 1600.
So--
Wonderful stories.
That's ironic, because my grandmother lived through World War I.
OK.
She went through so much suffering that I can't believe it.
And she still was-- she lost a brother before World War I.
She lost-- She went through World War I.
She lost two of her siblings, two of her children, and her little granddaughter.
My uncle from England lost a four-year-old child.
But she never lost her faith in God.
She was constantly religious.
And she used to tell me stories.
So what were the [INAUDIBLE]?
How hard war was.
How they became impoverished.
You know?
And war is hard.
And ironically, when the Nazi Germany invaded Poland, when they came marching in, my grandmother baked a cake.
And she said, [?
Itkala, ?] [NON-ENGLISH].
Carry it out for the poor soldiers.
She didn't know they came to kill us.
Well, it's not a question.
I heard September 1, 1939, sirens.
You know, airplanes were flying over.
And people started running.
And we lived not far from a street, which goes-- was the main street [INAUDIBLE].
And somebody had a-- a-- [INAUDIBLE].
I don't-- I had two addresses in my childhood.
One was [INAUDIBLE].
But I don't remember the exact number.
And this one, I don't remember, too.
I don't remember the address.
I never knew that it's going be one day important, so I didn't memorize those things.
Or, maybe they didn't make any special impressions.
But I remember we started running to hide.
And what I couldn't understand, my mother [? breathed ?] literature, loved literature, poetry, she could say poems.
In the bunker, she still [? started ?] to tell stories and to tell [? poetries ?] and tried to make it as normal as possible when we're there.
So when the Nazis invaded Poland, you know, they didn't start immediately.
First start, [? this ?] [? year. ?] They invaded Poland.
For us, the Holocaust started September 1, 1939.
And September 28, I believe, Warsaw was taken, the capital of Poland.
Till then, we were allowed to move from place to place.
In fact, people didn't know where to run.
Because, you see, you cannot always judge history by the past.
Because in the past, Russia had a far worst reputation for not being good to the Jews.
The [INAUDIBLE], you know, the [NON-ENGLISH] and others.
Excuse me.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] [DRINKS]
So people didn't know.
Some people would run to Russia-- I mean, would run-- would run to Warsaw, because it's a bigger city.
From Warsaw, people came.
People were running back and forth.
They didn't know.
And this I remember distinctly.
My father was always very protective of his family, because my-- his two half-sisters, [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE], they became orphans when they were very young.
And they lived with my Aunt [?
Sula ?] and with my Uncle [INAUDIBLE].
They were part of their household.
And they treated them like their own children.
Because we were a very close family, always.
We took in my grandmother.
They took in their sisters.
So my father said he has to go to Pultusk, because of his sister.
And we was going to run.
And my mother said something I never forget.
She said, the minute you start running, going from one place another-- she used the word, you become [NON-ENGLISH], which means, you no longer have a home.
You no longer have anything the minute you leave your home.
And we didn't know where to run.
And we didn't.
We didn't know what's happened.
Because once the Nazis invade-- took Warsaw, started to come down the systematic destruction, all kinds of laws.
So you're not allowed to any longer to travel from place to place.
They put up-- the first thing that they did-- so they go systematically.
Then, I'll tell you what they did, the first thing.
First thing, they came in, they round up all the intelligentsia.
[INAUDIBLE] Jewish--
Beg your pardon?
Well, I saw that they took some of the leaders away.
Yes.
And they took away the leaders, spiritual leaders, the intelligentsia, Polish, too, not just Jewish.
And I don't know what they did with them.
I didn't see what they did with them.
They took away.
Well, people say that they took them away and that they destroyed them.
But I didn't see--
I try only to tell you what I saw with my own eyes.
What I didn't see, what I heard, hearsay, I don't-- I just-- now, they started to burn the synagogues and to destroy the books.
And I was thinking, my mother valued so much the books.
And here, they burned books, the holy scriptures, all different yeshiva, the books.
They started to take away-- confiscate all our businesses.
We no longer were prohibited to go to school.
All social centers, curfews till certain hours for Jewish people, for the religious, was the hardest.
Because we no longer could have our [? minion ?] to observe the laws.
People would still try [? clandestine. ?] you know, but at the risk of their lives.
You know?
You were not congregating in large groups.
You had to give up all your valuables.
And you were not allowed to have any kind of transactions with non-- Germans, Jews and Germans.
It was dangerous for them, because they declared that we are an inferior people.
So it was [NON-ENGLISH], which means race-- it's like, it's not allowed.
And therefore, no kinds of transaction.
We would be forced to wear the yellow star.
[INAUDIBLE]--
I don't remember.
I know that we had to sew on, whether they distribute this-- I don't even remember how we got them.
But I know I had one in the front.
In some places, they had different, they tell me.
But in our city, we were wearing a yellow star in the front and a yellow star in the back.
We were not to walk on the pavement only in the-- only in the street.
As far as-- you know, whatever we did is under-- under the-- threat of death.
And we were not allowed to have any transactions with the Nazis, no-- not business, not social, not-- nothing, you know?
Because Hitler's law was to till the fourth, till the third generation he wanted to destroy us.
And we were not supposed to-- and I remember an incident that stuck very much in my memory.
One day, a civilian man we didn't know-- I don't know till this day-- was he a volksdeutsche?
Somebody who's German, but didn't live in Germany, that's called a volksdeutsche.
Or was he a disguised Nazi?
Who he was, I don't know.
But he came to our yard.
And he was selling some goods.
But most people didn't have money.
This was at the very beginning, the very beginning.
So everybody was standing.
But people were looking, because we had to give up our valuables.
And we were rationed.
You know, stand in long lines.
And we're always getting [INAUDIBLE].
But anyhow, we had to give up valuable.
And if they would find that you had something left, even though it was yours, you would be shot.
So everything was punishable to death.
You know, without provocation they did that, but especially.
So as everybody was staying there, all of a sudden, it dawned on my mother, we're not supposed to talk with somebody who is not Jewish.
You know? and we don't know who he is, because if he would be a Jewish person, he wouldn't have all this merchandise and openly dealing.
And-- excuse me.
One person walked over that came from the street.
And he saw everybody in a circle.
He came over.
His name was Kalfus.
This was the man that he owned the school.
And he, when my mother saw that everybody staying there on the yard, she said, if somebody is going to walk by, they going to get in trouble.
So she said, please, come into our house.
She invited everybody in.
And hardly anybody bought everything, because I know my family didn't give up all the money.
And I'll tell you a little later how-- what they did.
But most people gave up.
And we gave up most of the valuables and money and whatever we had.
So when she invited-- but Kalfus, for some reason, he was a well-to-do man before the war.
I know the building from the school was his.
What else he had, I don't know.
They were not personal friends.
We knew-- I knew of him.
And he bought most of the merchandise.
And when he left-- it didn't take long-- the same men came with the Gestapo.
He denounced us and brought them to our home.
Because all the others he didn't know.
So they started to interrogate my mother.
Who is the man?
And that was the first time, in my presence at least, that I heard my mother lying.
She said, I don't know.
Because at that time, we already realized that they use something that if one would do something wrong, they would destroy the whole family, sometimes punish everybody, one for all.
So my mother realized that if she's going to tell, that they're going to kill the whole family.
Because they broke the laws imposed by the Nazis.
So she continued to say she doesn't know.
They interrogate her for a long time.
But she said, no.
So they saw that they're not getting any place with her, that she is resisting.
They took her to the police station.
Were you in the same room with her when this was going on?
No.
Listen, when they took my mother, I was afraid.
I already knew.
I was a 13-year-old girl.
I already saw what's going on.
I saw that they're not our friends and not the same Nazis-- Germans that my grandmother used to talk about.
So I followed her.
So they kept on chasing me away.
And finally, one of them got a bright idea, said, [NON-ENGLISH].
You see, that's why it was useful that I spoke Yiddish.
I could understand what they said more, because it's similar.
And mostly during the Holocaust, I could follow all this.
And that saved me from a lot of beating.
So we went.
They took us both.
They brought us to the Gestapo station.
And they separated us.
My mother--
